steps.reveal: post multos dies = after many days (acc. with 'post')

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'multi dies' — 'multos dies' with -os is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'multis diebus' — 'post' takes the accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'multis diebus' — 'post' governs the accusative

steps.reveal: congregatis omnibus = all things having been gathered together

hints.ablative of means: Ablative of means would answer 'by what' — this is a complete ablative absolute clause
hints.dative of reference: 'Congregatis omnibus' could look dative, but as a participle + noun in ablative it is an ablative absolute
hints.ablative of manner: Ablative of manner describes how — this ablative absolute describes a circumstance

hints.to separate: 'To separate' is 'segregare' — 'congregare' means to gather together
hints.to greet: 'To greet' is 'salutare' — 'congregare' means to gather together

hints.grego, gregare, gregavi, gregatum: 'Gregare' is extremely rare — 'congregare' is the standard compound
hints.segrego, segregare, segregavi, segregatu: These are the parts of 'segregare' (to separate) — opposite meaning
hints.aggrego, aggregare, aggregavi, aggregatu: These are the parts of 'aggregare' (to add to a flock) — different prefix

steps.reveal: filius iunior = the younger son (nom., subject of 'profectus est')

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'filium iuniorem' — 'filius iunior' is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'filio iuniore' — 'filius iunior' is nominative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'filii iunioris' — 'filius iunior' is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'filii' ends in -ii/-i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'filii' ends in -ii
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'filii' ends in -ii

steps.prompt: 'Iunior' is what form of the adjective?

hints.positive (young): Positive would be 'iuvenis' — 'iunior' with -ior is comparative
hints.superlative (youngest): Superlative would be 'iunissimus' or 'minimus natu' — 'iunior' is comparative
hints.irregular positive: 'Iunior' is not a positive form — the -ior ending marks it as comparative

steps.reveal: peregre = abroad (adverb of motion to a foreign place)

hints.on foot: 'On foot' would be 'pedibus' — 'peregre' means to/in a foreign land

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'profectus est' translates to?

hints.to make progress: 'To make progress' is 'proficere' — 'proficisci' is a deponent meaning to set out
hints.to carry forward: 'To carry forward' is 'proferre' — 'proficisci' means to set out on a journey

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'profectus est'?
steps.reveal: profectus est = he departed / he set out

hints.perfect passive indicative: 'Proficisci' is deponent — passive in form but active in meaning, so this is perfect active
hints.present passive indicative: Present would be 'proficiscitur' — 'profectus est' is perfect
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'profectus erat' — 'est' makes this perfect

hints.proficio, proficere, profeci, profectum: These are the parts of 'proficere' (to make progress) — different verb
hints.confiteor, confiteri, confessus sum: These are the parts of 'confiteri' (to confess) — different deponent
hints.patior, pati, passus sum: These are the parts of 'pati' (to suffer) — different deponent

steps.prompt: This verse begins which famous parable?
steps.target: the Parable of the Prodigal Son
choices: the Parable of the Prodigal Son
the Parable of the Lost Sheep
the Parable of the Good Samaritan

hints.the Parable of the Lost Sheep: The Lost Sheep is earlier in Luke 15 — this is about the younger son departing
hints.the Parable of the Talents: The Talents is in Matthew 25 — this is Luke 15's Prodigal Son
hints.the Parable of the Good Samaritan: The Good Samaritan is Luke 10 — this is about the younger son going abroad

translation: And not many days later, having gathered all his things, the younger son departed to a foreign land
